questions.question: Which tag creates an unordered (bulleted) list?
questions.explanation: The <ul> (unordered list) tag creates a bulleted list. Each item inside is wrapped in an <li> (list item) tag. By default, browsers render bullets as filled circles.

questions.question: What is the correct structure for a definition list?
questions.explanation: A definition list uses <dl> as the container, <dt> (definition term) for the term being defined, and <dd> (definition description) for the definition or description.

questions.question: How do you create a nested list in HTML?
choices: Place a <ul> or <ol> directly inside the parent <ul>
Place a <ul> or <ol> inside an <li> element of the parent list
Use the nested attribute on the <li> tag
Indent the <li> elements with extra spaces
questions.explanation: To nest a list, place a complete <ul> or <ol> inside an <li> element of the parent list. The nested list becomes a child of that list item.

questions.question: Which attribute changes the starting number of an ordered list?
questions.explanation: The start attribute on an <ol> tag sets the starting number for the list. For example, <ol start="5"> begins numbering at 5.

questions.question: The ___ tag is used to wrap each individual item in both ordered and unordered lists.
questions.explanation: The <li> (list item) tag defines each entry in an ordered (<ol>) or unordered (<ul>) list. It is the only valid direct child of <ol> and <ul> elements.

questions.question: To make an ordered list count in reverse, add the ___ attribute to the <ol> tag.
questions.explanation: The reversed attribute on an <ol> tag makes the list count downward. For example, a three-item list would be numbered 3, 2, 1 instead of 1, 2, 3.
